scenario.architecture_name: He uses self-work and poly boundary language to turn shared responsibility into a threat to his autonomy, making ordinary questions feel like control.
scenario.demographics: F/28 Lakota descent in Rapid City; M/29 from Denver, attended college in Boulder, gaming hobby shaped by time spent in Japan.
scenario.cultural_context: The narrator reads care through Lakota emphasis on community, showing up, and shared responsibility; her partner brings a more individualistic Western frame and borrows polyamorous language about growth, agreements, and boundaries to protect his personal freedom.
core_behaviors: Frames ordinary accountability questions as interrogation or control
Uses growth and boundary language to make his preferences sound principled
Accepts her community-minded support while keeping his own spending and choices separate
scenario.satellite_signals: Polyamorous self-work vocabulary; gaming and gacha spending minimized as identity and joy; mobility asymmetry because she lacks a car; cultural contrast between shared responsibility and individual autonomy.
scenario.dismissible_red_flags: The language sounds emotionally aware and modern; his gaming purchase is still small; his praise for her community values can be read as genuine appreciation.
scenario.scenario_text:
I’ve been seeing Eli for almost three months, and honestly it’s been good. He’s 29, from Denver, went to Boulder, and works remote in Rapid City. We met through my cousin’s fundraiser, and he stayed after to fold chairs. In my family, if people are cleaning up, you clean up. He said he loved how "your aunties all show up for each other" and that "that kind of community is beautiful, but I had to learn to be my own person." I thought that was honest.

He listens to poly podcasts even though he says he’s "only seeing me right now." He showed me a relationship menu on his phone and said, "I want to grow with you, not box you in." Some of the language is new to me, like when he says jealousy is just information, but I like that he wants to talk instead of assuming.

The only little thing was Saturday. I don’t have a car this winter, so he usually drives and chooses the place. We were at his apartment after I bought groceries for wojapi and soup because his check was late. He was showing me a Japanese game he got into when he spent time in Japan, and he bought a third round because the banner was almost gone. I asked, kind of joking, "How much is a round?" He grinned and called it research.

Then he squeezed my knee and said, "My boundary is that I don’t get interrogated, babe." I laughed because it sounded like therapist language, and he added, "I’m a 29-year-old man. I’m allowed to have little joys." He said he’d Venmo me when his check cleared, and later during our state-of-the-union he said, "You’re not controlling like that. You get me." That felt sweet, mostly. I just got quiet for a second.

If he had asked why I spent money, I think I would explain it, but I don’t want to act like I’m keeping score. Is it weird that the "interrogated" comment stuck with me a little?

snippets.snippet_text: from Denver, went to Boulder
snippets.reasoning: Geographic and class positioning. Denver and Boulder place him in a different cultural lane from her Rapid City family network.

snippets.snippet_text: he stayed after to fold chairs
snippets.reasoning: A helpful community gesture wins trust in her value system. It may be sincere, but it also quickly establishes him as someone who understands how to be approved of.

snippets.snippet_text: if people are cleaning up, you clean up
snippets.reasoning: This names her community framework: responsibility is shared, practical, and visible. It sets up why his later separation of his choices from shared impact lands oddly.

snippets.snippet_text: your aunties all show up for each other
snippets.reasoning: Complimenting her aunties speaks directly to her family values. The phrasing also lets him praise the community as something beautiful without necessarily joining its obligations.

snippets.snippet_text: I had to learn to be my own person
snippets.reasoning: Individualism is framed as self-knowledge. In this context, it quietly contrasts with her shared-responsibility ethic and prepares the idea that asking about impact could threaten his identity.

snippets.snippet_text: only seeing me right now
snippets.reasoning: The reassurance contains an expiration hatch. “Right now” softens her concern while leaving future changes preauthorized.

snippets.snippet_text: relationship menu
snippets.reasoning: A poly infrastructure tool can be healthy when mutual. Here it arrives very early and gives him a formal framework for defining the relationship before she fully knows the terms.

snippets.snippet_text: I want to grow with you, not box you in
snippets.reasoning: Growth language makes his framework sound liberating. The risk is that later discomfort can be treated as refusing growth rather than responding to his choices.

snippets.snippet_text: jealousy is just information
snippets.reasoning: In poly language, this can mean feelings are worth examining. Used by someone avoiding accountability, it can relocate the problem from what he did to what she needs to process.

snippets.snippet_text: instead of assuming
snippets.reasoning: She is already translating unfamiliar language into maturity. That makes it harder for her to notice when the same language blocks normal questions.

snippets.snippet_text: I don’t have a car this winter
snippets.reasoning: Mobility gap. Her lack of a car makes his driving feel generous while also giving him more control over where they go and when.

snippets.snippet_text: he usually drives and chooses the place
snippets.reasoning: Care and control overlap here. Driving helps her, but the repeated pattern means he controls the logistics and she is moved through his plans.

snippets.snippet_text: I bought groceries for wojapi and soup because his check was late
snippets.reasoning: She steps in materially when his money is unavailable, and the food reference grounds that help in her care tradition. The imbalance matters because he soon protects his own discretionary spending from question.

snippets.snippet_text: a Japanese game he got into when he spent time in Japan
snippets.reasoning: This places the gaming hobby inside his identity and past experience, making it easier for spending on the game to be framed as personal expression.

snippets.snippet_text: he bought a third round because the banner was almost gone
snippets.reasoning: Scarcity-driven spending. The purchase is small at this stage, but the logic is urgency and reward, not shared budgeting.

snippets.snippet_text: How much is a round?
snippets.reasoning: Her question is ordinary and concrete. The later reaction depends on making this small financial curiosity seem invasive.

snippets.snippet_text: He grinned and called it research
snippets.reasoning: He jokes the purchase into something smarter and less discussable. The grin keeps it light while sidestepping the actual cost.

snippets.snippet_text: My boundary is that I don’t get interrogated, babe
snippets.reasoning: Boundary language is used as a shield against a normal question. Calling it interrogation changes her role from partner asking about money to someone violating him.

snippets.snippet_text: I laughed because it sounded like therapist language
snippets.reasoning: She notices the language is oddly formal for the moment, then laughs it off. Her body resolves the tension before the question gets answered.

snippets.snippet_text: I’m a 29-year-old man. I’m allowed to have little joys.
snippets.reasoning: His age and identity become the answer to a money question. “Little joys” minimizes the spending and makes her concern sound like denying him personhood.

snippets.snippet_text: he’d Venmo me when his check cleared
snippets.reasoning: Repayment is deferred while his game spending is immediate. The promise may be genuine, but the timing leaves her carrying the practical cost.

snippets.snippet_text: our state-of-the-union
snippets.reasoning: A structured check-in can be healthy. Here, the formal container lets him rename the event after the fact and reward the interpretation he prefers.

snippets.snippet_text: You’re not controlling like that. You get me.
snippets.reasoning: Compliment as role assignment. She is praised for not being controlling, which makes future questions risk losing the special status of being the woman who understands him.

snippets.snippet_text: I don’t want to act like I’m keeping score
snippets.reasoning: She talks herself out of noticing imbalance. The concern is fairness, but she reframes fairness as pettiness before he has to address it.
